blocks.text: Chapter 6.
Overconfidence: Illusions, Formulas, and the Premortem
The ideas in 30 seconds
Our sense that we understand the past is largely an illusion built by coherent stories, and it breeds unjustified confidence that we can predict the future.

Subjective confidence is a feeling produced by the coherence of a story, not evidence of accuracy — whole professions (stock picking, political punditry) sustain skilled-feeling performance that is statistically indistinguishable from chance.
In low-validity environments, simple formulas and checklists consistently match or beat expert judgment — Paul Meehl's finding, replicated in 200+ studies, and embodied in the Apgar score.
Expert intuition is trustworthy only when two conditions hold: a regular, predictable environment and prolonged practice with rapid, clear feedback.
The planning fallacy makes us budget for the best case; the cure is the outside view — anchoring forecasts on the statistics of similar past projects (reference-class forecasting).
Optimism is the engine of capitalism and the source of its wrecks; Gary Klein's premortem is a cheap ritual that legitimizes doubt before commitment.
Hindsight Manufactures the Illusion of Understanding

Nassim Taleb's narrative fallacy names our habit of compressing the messy past into tidy cause-and-effect stories that overweight talent and intention and underweight luck. The Google founding legend feels inevitable in retrospect, yet at one point Larry Page and Sergey Brin tried to sell the company for under $1 million and the buyer declined — a story of luck retold as a story of genius. The better a story explains, the less we notice what it leaves out; a compelling narrative is a WYSIATI product, coherent precisely because it is simple.

Baruch Fischhoff demonstrated hindsight bias experimentally: before Nixon's 1972 trips to China and the USSR, subjects assigned probabilities to possible outcomes; afterward, they misremembered their own estimates as closer to what actually happened. Once we adopt a new view of the world, we cannot reconstruct our old beliefs — "I knew it all along" is a systematic memory distortion, not a boast.

Hindsight produces outcome bias: we judge decisions by their results rather than by the quality of the reasoning available at the time. Officials who took sensible precautions against a disaster that never came look paranoid; those who missed a low-probability catastrophe look negligent. This punishes prudent risk-takers, rewards reckless gamblers who got lucky, and drives professionals such as physicians toward defensive, bureaucratic decision-making.

The same machinery inflates leadership narratives. Because a CEO's actual influence on firm performance is modest — a correlation of roughly .30, meaning the stronger of two CEOs leads the stronger firm about 60% of the time rather than 50% — the confident causal stories in books like Built to Last mostly repackage the halo effect plus luck. Predictably, the performance gap between celebrated and comparison firms shrinks toward zero afterward: regression to the mean, dressed up as rise and fall.
Confidence Is a Feeling, Not a Measurement — the Illusion of Validity

As a young psychologist, Kahneman evaluated Israeli army officer candidates using a leaderless-group obstacle test. He and his colleagues formed vivid, confident impressions of who would lead — yet feedback from officer school showed their forecasts were barely better than blind guesses. Knowing this changed nothing: the next batch of candidates produced the same vivid certainty. He named this the illusion of validity — confidence generated by the coherence of an impression, wholly insensitive to evidence that the impression predicts nothing.

Finance offers the cleanest demonstration. Terry Odean analyzed 10,000 individual brokerage accounts: on average, the shares investors sold went on to outperform the shares they bought by about 3.2 percentage points per year — trading destroyed value beyond costs. Barber and Odean's follow-up, aptly titled "Trading Is Hazardous to Your Wealth," showed the most active traders earned the worst returns, and men (who trade more) did worse than women.

Professionals fare no better. Year-to-year persistence in mutual-fund performance is essentially zero; when Kahneman computed rank correlations of investment advisers' results across eight years at a wealth-management firm, the average was .01 — the bonuses rewarded luck, and the firm quietly ignored the finding. This is the illusion of skill: an entire industry's culture sustains beliefs that its own data refute, because everyone around you shares them.

Philip Tetlock's landmark study of expert political and economic prediction — roughly 80,000 forecasts — found experts performed worse than simple base-rate algorithms and barely better than chance, while remaining supremely confident. Confident, single-big-idea "hedgehogs" made the worst and most publicized predictions; doubt-tolerant "foxes" did somewhat better. The lesson generalizes: in an unpredictable world, errors of prediction are inevitable, but sincere high confidence in long-range forecasts is a warning sign, not a credential.
When Judgment Is Needed, Prefer the Formula

In 1954, Paul Meehl reviewed studies pitting trained clinicians against simple statistical rules built from a few scores. The rules matched or beat the experts, and the result has now been replicated in more than 200 domains — medical prognoses, credit risk, parole recidivism, foster-parent success, wine prices. Experts lose because they attempt clever, complex judgments (usually reducing validity), and because human judgment is noisy: shown the same information twice, experts often contradict themselves, and a formula never does.

Economist Orley Ashenfelter predicts Bordeaux vintage quality from three weather variables — summer temperature, harvest rain, and previous-winter rain — with a correlation above .90, outperforming expert tasters who swirl and sniff. Robyn Dawes added a liberating twist: improper linear models with equal weights on sensibly chosen variables do about as well as optimally fitted regressions. You do not need historical data to build a useful formula — you need a handful of relevant dimensions and the discipline to score them.

The iconic success is Virginia Apgar's 1953 score: five observable signs of a newborn (heart rate, respiration, reflex, muscle tone, color), each rated 0-2, applied minutes after birth. It replaced obstetricians' varying intuitions with a standard, saved countless infants, and remains in daily use.

Resistance to algorithms is fierce — Meehl was distressed by clinicians' hostility — and asymmetric: an algorithm's error feels intolerable while a human's identical error feels natural. Kahneman's practical compromise for hiring: choose up to six prerequisite traits, ask factual questions and score each trait 1-5 independently, then commit in advance to hiring the candidate with the highest total. Resist the urge to override the sum with a gut feeling; disciplined scoring plus a final structured judgment beats an unstructured interview.
Two Conditions Separate Real Expertise From Confident Noise

Gary Klein built his career studying the genuine intuitive brilliance of fireground commanders and nurses — the naturalistic-decision-making school. Kahneman built his studying the failures of expert judgment. Their adversarial collaboration asked when intuition can be trusted, and they converged on an answer rather than a winner.

The foundation is Herbert Simon's account of skill, summarized in his line that intuition is "nothing more and nothing less than recognition." A chess master "sees" a strong move the way you read a word: a cue retrieves stored patterns from memory. Intuition is pattern recognition, so it is only as good as the patterns the environment allowed you to learn.

Two conditions must both hold. First, the environment must be sufficiently regular to be predictable — stable relationships between cues and outcomes. Second, the practitioner must have had prolonged practice with clear, rapid feedback, the apprenticeship needed to learn those regularities. Chess, firefighting, anesthesiology, and clinical nursing qualify. Stock picking and long-term political forecasting fail the first condition — the environment is close to random — so experience there breeds only confidence, never skill. Radiologists and psychotherapists occupy a middle ground: valid intuitions about some judgments (reading an image, sensing a patient's immediate state) and worthless ones about others (long-term prognosis).

The operational rule: never assess an intuition by the confidence with which it is delivered, including your own. Ask instead whether the environment is regular and whether the judge had adequate opportunity to learn it. If either answer is no, the intuition is a System 1 story wearing the costume of expertise — and a checklist or formula deserves the final word.
The Planning Fallacy and the Outside View

Kahneman lived the planning fallacy himself. A team he led to write a decision-making curriculum estimated completion in about two years. He then asked a curriculum expert on the team what had happened to similar teams: roughly 40% never finished, and none had finished in under seven years. The team acknowledged the statistic — and kept working to its two-year plan. The book took eight years and was never used.

The episode isolates two ways to forecast. The inside view builds a prediction from the specifics of your case — your plan, your team, your progress — and naturally simulates the best-case scenario while ignoring the unknown unknowns. The outside view treats your project as one instance of a reference class and starts from that class's statistics. The inside view feels more informed; the outside view is more accurate.

The base rates are grim and universal. The Scottish Parliament building was estimated in 1997 at up to £40 million and completed in 2004 for about £431 million. Bent Flyvbjerg's worldwide database of rail projects found average cost overruns above 40%, with ridership overestimated in some 90% of cases — and no improvement over 30 years, because each new team takes the inside view. American homeowners expecting kitchen remodels to cost about $18,700 paid about $38,800 on average.

Flyvbjerg's remedy, reference-class forecasting, is now mandated for large British public projects: (1) identify the appropriate reference class of comparable projects; (2) obtain its distribution of outcomes (cost overrun percentages, completion times) as your baseline prediction; (3) adjust from that baseline only with specific, defensible reasons your case differs. Note the organizational wrinkle: overruns are not always innocent errors. Planners often lowball deliberately to win approval, knowing sunk costs will carry the project forward — so the discipline must be imposed by the people paying, not the people proposing.
Optimism Is an Engine — Add a Premortem Before You Ship

Optimistic bias may be the most consequential bias of all, because optimists make the decisions that shape economies: they found companies, take risks, persist through setbacks, and recover from failure. Optimism is partly temperament, largely heritable, and correlated with health and resilience. But its cost is systematic miscalibration. About 35% of American small businesses survive five years, yet founders put the chance of success for "a business like yours" at 60% — and 81% rated their own odds at 7 in 10 or better, with a third assigning failure a probability of zero.

Evidence rarely helps. The Canadian Inventor's Assistance Program gave inventors objective ratings of their ideas; among those graded at the failing level, 47% kept investing anyway and roughly doubled their losses before quitting. Competition neglect compounds the error: entrants judge their prospects by their own abilities and plans, ignoring that rivals — equally optimistic — are entering the same market, which is why waves of restaurants open into locations where restaurants routinely die.

Even elite professionals are wildly overconfident about uncertainty itself. Duke University collected over 11,000 one-year S&P 500 forecasts from corporate CFOs: their estimates correlated slightly negatively with actual returns, and their 80% confidence intervals contained the true outcome only about a third of the time — intervals honest enough to be right 80% of the time would have been so wide as to embarrass the forecaster. Organizations reward confident narrowness and punish honest uncertainty, so overconfidence persists.

Gary Klein's premortem is the low-cost partial cure. When a decision is nearly final but not yet committed, gather the knowledgeable group and announce: imagine it is one year from now, we implemented the plan, and it was a disaster — take ten minutes and write that disaster's history. The exercise legitimizes doubt just when group loyalty is suppressing it, rewards imaginative skeptics instead of marginalizing them, and surfaces failure modes while they can still be fixed.
In practice
Before your next big estimate, find the reference class first: ask "how did projects like this actually turn out?" and adjust from that base rate, not from your plan.
Run a ten-minute premortem on any plan nearing commitment: have each stakeholder write a one-page history of how it failed a year from now.
Replace one unstructured judgment this week with a simple score: define up to six relevant traits, rate each 1-5 independently, and let the total decide.
Before trusting an expert (including yourself), apply the two-condition test: Is the environment regular enough to learn? Has the judge had years of practice with fast, clear feedback?
Evaluate a recent decision by the information available when it was made, not by its outcome — and insist others judge yours the same way.
Widen every confidence interval you state or hear; assume 80% intervals from confident professionals capture the truth only about a third of the time.
